past_medical_history: Polycystic ovary syndrome diagnosed at age 17
family_history: Mother: type 2 diabetes mellitus, systemic lupus erythematosus
No family history of epilepsy or psychiatric illness

social_history.alcohol: Rare social drinking, less than once monthly
social_history.recreational_drugs: Denies illicit drug use, denies marijuana
social_history.living_situation: Lives in university dormitory with a roommate

neurological_exam.mental_status: Patient is drowsy but arousable to voice, oriented only to self. She is unable to state the date, month, year, or current location. Speech is markedly reduced in fluency with frequent paraphasic errors and perseveration. She intermittently becomes agitated and attempts to remove her IV lines. She is unable to follow two-step commands. Attention is severely impaired; she cannot spell WORLD backward or perform serial 7s. Short-term memory is non-testable as she cannot register three words. Intermittent episodes of staring with lip-smacking and subtle right arm posturing are observed, lasting 10-20 seconds each.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal and reactive at 3 mm bilaterally. Extraocular movements conjugate without nystagmus. Corneal reflexes intact bilaterally. Facial sensation intact to noxious stimuli symmetrically. Prominent orofacial dyskinesias with repetitive lip-smacking, tongue protrusion, and chewing movements are near-continuous. Hearing grossly intact to voice. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength intact. Tongue midline when briefly protruded.
neurological_exam.motor: Tone is increased in bilateral upper extremities with cogwheel quality at the wrists. Intermittent dystonic posturing of the right hand with wrist flexion and finger extension. Strength is approximately 4+/5 in all four extremities when patient cooperates, though examination is limited by encephalopathy and involuntary movements. No clear lateralizing weakness.
neurological_exam.sensory: Withdraws briskly and symmetrically to noxious stimuli in all four extremities. Detailed sensory examination not possible due to altered mental status.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 3+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Bilateral plantar responses flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing not reliably performed due to encephalopathy and involuntary choreiform movements of the upper extremities.
neurological_exam.gait: Not assessed; patient is bed-bound with continuous monitoring due to altered mental status and seizure risk.
neurological_exam.additional: Prominent continuous orofacial dyskinesias with stereotyped lip-smacking and chewing movements. Choreiform movements of bilateral upper extremities, more pronounced on the right. Episodic autonomic instability manifested by heart rate fluctuations between 60 and 140 bpm during the encounter, diaphoresis, and one episode of transient hypertension to 185/102 mmHg. Three discrete episodes of behavioral arrest with subtle right arm tonic posturing lasting 10-20 seconds were observed, highly suspicious for subclinical seizures.

patient.chief_complaint: Worsening confusion, seizures, and involuntary movements over 10 days
patient.history_present_illness: This 21-year-old right-handed Hispanic woman is brought to the emergency department by ambulance after her university roommate called 911 for a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure. According to her roommate and her mother who arrived from out of town two days ago, the patient began acting strangely approximately two and a half weeks ago. She initially developed what appeared to be a mild flu-like illness with headache, low-grade fever, body aches, and fatigue lasting three to four days. She seemed to recover but then began exhibiting personality changes that her roommate described as completely out of character. She became unusually suspicious of friends, accused her roommate of going through her belongings, and withdrew from social activities. She stopped attending classes and was found by her roommate talking to herself in their shared room on multiple occasions. Her mother was contacted approximately one week ago because the roommate was concerned. Her mother drove up and noted that her daughter appeared confused, was sleeping only two to three hours per night, and had difficulty remembering recent conversations. The mother took her to a campus urgent care clinic six days ago, where she was evaluated by a physician assistant who suspected a first episode of psychosis and prescribed olanzapine 5 mg nightly. The patient took two doses but her condition continued to worsen. She developed involuntary repetitive lip-smacking and tongue movements that her mother initially attributed to the new medication. Four days prior to this presentation, she had her first witnessed seizure at home, described as whole-body stiffening followed by rhythmic jerking of all extremities lasting roughly two minutes. EMS was called and she was taken to a local community hospital where a non-contrast CT head was normal and basic labs were unremarkable aside from a sodium of 133 mEq/L. She was given levetiracetam 500 mg IV and discharged with instructions to follow up with neurology. Over the following days, the dyskinesias persisted and worsened despite discontinuation of olanzapine. She became increasingly confused and had two more seizure-like episodes characterized by staring and right arm stiffening. Today she had a prolonged generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting approximately three minutes, prompting the current presentation to this tertiary academic medical center emergency department.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: No identifiable posterior dominant rhythm; alpha activity is absent
background.overall_organization: Severely disorganized with loss of normal anterior-posterior gradient
background.continuity: Continuous with intermittent periods of attenuation lasting 2-5 seconds

findings.location: Generalized, maximal over bilateral frontocentral regions (F3, F4, C3, C4)
findings.frequency: 1-3 Hz delta activity with superimposed 20-25 Hz beta bursts
findings.morphology: High-amplitude rhythmic delta waves (150-250 microvolts) with superimposed fast beta activity giving a characteristic brush-like appearance
findings.state: Present continuously during wakefulness and persisting into drowsiness
findings.clinical_correlation: Extreme delta brush is a unique EEG pattern that is pathognomonic for anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis

findings.location: Right temporal onset (T4/T6) with rapid bilateral spread
findings.frequency: Three electrographic seizures captured during 40-minute recording, each lasting 30-90 seconds
findings.morphology: Rhythmic 5-7 Hz theta activity evolving to repetitive sharp-and-slow-wave complexes
findings.state: Associated with subtle clinical correlate of behavioral arrest and right arm tonic posturing
findings.clinical_correlation: Frequent subclinical seizures indicating significant epileptogenic burden requiring aggressive anti-seizure management
Indicates diffuse cortical dysfunction consistent with encephalitis

artifacts.description: Intermittent frontalis and temporalis muscle artifact related to orofacial dyskinesias and jaw movements
Occasional electrode artifact from choreiform upper extremity movements

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed; patient unable to cooperate with instructions
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Performed at standard frequencies (1-30 Hz); no photic driving response; no photoparoxysmal response

eeg.impression: Severely abnormal EEG demonstrating the extreme delta brush pattern, which is pathognomonic for anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. Three electrographic seizures of right temporal origin were captured with subtle clinical correlates. Continuous generalized rhythmic delta activity reflects severe diffuse cortical dysfunction. Continuous EEG monitoring is strongly recommended given the high seizure burden.
eeg.limitations: Recording duration limited to 40 minutes; continuous EEG monitoring is essential to quantify full seizure burden and guide anti-seizure therapy
recommended_actions: Initiate continuous EEG monitoring for a minimum of 24-48 hours
Optimize anti-seizure medication given documented electrographic seizures
Urgent CSF analysis with anti-NMDA receptor antibody panel
Brain MRI with contrast to evaluate for structural correlates

findings.location: Bilateral medial temporal lobes including hippocampi and amygdalae, symmetric
findings.size: Right: 1.8 x 1.2 cm, Left: 1.9 x 1.3 cm

additional_observations: Normal cervical spine alignment on sagittal sequences
mri.impression: Bilateral symmetric medial temporal lobe FLAIR hyperintensities involving the hippocampi and amygdalae without restricted diffusion, hemorrhage, or pathologic enhancement. Subtle bilateral cingulate gyrus signal abnormality is also noted. The bilateral symmetric distribution without hemorrhage or restricted diffusion strongly favors autoimmune encephalitis over herpes simplex encephalitis. Recommend CSF analysis with autoimmune encephalitis antibody panel.

differential_by_imaging.rationale: Bilateral symmetric medial temporal FLAIR signal without hemorrhage, restricted diffusion, or enhancement is characteristic of autoimmune encephalitis
HSV typically causes asymmetric temporal lobe involvement with hemorrhage, restricted diffusion, and often insular/frontal extension; none of these features are present
Cannot be reliably distinguished from antibody-mediated autoimmune encephalitis by imaging alone; requires serologic testing
Can cause temporal lobe signal changes but clinical context makes this unlikely

recommended_actions: CSF analysis with comprehensive autoimmune encephalitis antibody panel
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis for occult neoplasm if autoimmune encephalitis confirmed
Follow-up MRI in 2-4 weeks to monitor treatment response

findings: Non-specific T-wave flattening in leads V1-V3
ecg.interpretation: Sinus tachycardia at 118 bpm with non-specific T-wave flattening in the anterior leads. No acute ischemic changes. QTc at upper limit of normal at 448 ms.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Tachycardia is consistent with autonomic instability in the setting of encephalitis, low-grade fever, and agitation. QTc should be monitored, especially if antipsychotics or other QTc-prolonging agents are considered.

BMP.clinical_significance: Hyponatremia, consistent with SIADH in the setting of CNS inflammation
Low, consistent with hypotonic hyponatremia and SIADH
Mildly elevated, likely stress response or related to metformin non-compliance during illness

Other.clinical_significance: Elevated, likely due to repeated seizure activity and rigidity

labs.interpretation: Significant hyponatremia at 129 mEq/L with low chloride, strongly suggestive of SIADH in the context of CNS disease. CK is elevated at 245 U/L, attributable to recent seizure activity and increased muscle tone. Mildly elevated CRP and ESR indicate a low-grade inflammatory process. Thyroid function is normal, arguing against Hashimoto encephalopathy. Hepatic and renal function are preserved. Urine drug screen is negative, effectively ruling out substance-induced symptoms. Pregnancy has been excluded.

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Positive (5 unique bands in CSF, absent in serum)
special_tests.Cytology: Activated lymphocytes, no malignant cells identified

csf.interpretation: Lymphocytic pleocytosis (78 cells/uL, 93% lymphocytes) with elevated protein and mildly elevated opening pressure. Normal glucose ratio excludes bacterial meningitis. Oligoclonal bands and markedly elevated IgG index indicate robust intrathecal immunoglobulin synthesis. All infectious PCRs are negative. Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies are positive at high titer (1:256) in the CSF, establishing the diagnosis of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis.

NMDAR Antibody Panel.clinical_significance: Serum positivity confirms systemic antibody production, though CSF is more sensitive and has higher titer
High CSF titer with intrathecal production confirms anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis

output.interpretation: Both serum and CSF anti-NMDA receptor antibodies are positive. The CSF titer (1:256) is substantially higher than the serum titer (1:80), demonstrating intrathecal antibody synthesis, which is the hallmark of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. This pattern confirms the diagnosis and distinguishes it from incidental serum positivity.

Pelvic Ultrasound.value: 4.1 x 3.0 x 2.8 cm, mildly enlarged with peripheral follicular pattern consistent with known PCOS. No focal mass lesion identified.
Pelvic Ultrasound.clinical_significance: Polycystic morphology consistent with patient's known PCOS; no teratoma

Pelvic Ultrasound.value: 3.8 x 2.5 x 2.4 cm. Contains a 2.3 cm well-circumscribed complex mass with mixed echogenicity including hyperechoic foci with posterior acoustic shadowing (calcification) and internal fat-fluid level. Internal vascularity on Doppler is minimal.
Pelvic Ultrasound.clinical_significance: Complex ovarian mass with fat and calcification highly consistent with mature cystic teratoma (dermoid cyst); known trigger for anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis in young women

Pelvic Ultrasound.value: Normal size (7.2 x 4.1 x 3.8 cm), anteverted, homogeneous myometrium, endometrial stripe 8 mm
Small amount of physiologic free fluid in the posterior cul-de-sac

output.interpretation: A 2.3 cm complex left ovarian mass with fat, calcification, and a fat-fluid level is highly consistent with a mature cystic teratoma (dermoid cyst). This finding is clinically critical as ovarian teratomas containing neural tissue can express NMDA receptors and trigger the autoimmune response underlying anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. Surgical resection of the teratoma is recommended as it improves neurological outcomes and significantly reduces relapse risk. The right ovary shows polycystic morphology consistent with the patient's known PCOS history.
abnormal_values_summary: Left ovary: 2.3 cm complex mass consistent with mature cystic teratoma

Autoimmune Encephalitis Panel.clinical_significance: Low-titer ANA is nonspecific and commonly seen in autoimmune conditions; does not indicate lupus cerebritis in this clinical context given maternal SLE history

output.interpretation: The comprehensive autoimmune encephalitis antibody panel confirms anti-NMDA receptor antibody positivity in CSF. All other neuronal surface antibodies are negative, excluding overlap syndromes. Anti-TPO is normal, ruling out Hashimoto encephalopathy. A low-titer ANA (1:80) is noted but is nonspecific and does not support a diagnosis of CNS lupus in this clinical setting. The findings are consistent with isolated anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis.

signal_characteristics.density: Mixed density with fat attenuation (-55 HU), soft tissue component, and punctate calcifications
signal_characteristics.enhancement: No significant enhancement of the mass

findings.mass_effect: None; no invasion of adjacent structures

additional_observations: No pulmonary nodules, masses, or consolidation
Right ovary appears mildly enlarged with multiple peripheral follicles, consistent with PCOS
output.impression: A 2.4 cm well-circumscribed left ovarian mass containing fat, soft tissue, and calcification, consistent with a mature cystic teratoma (dermoid cyst). No evidence of malignant features such as invasion or lymphadenopathy. No other primary malignancy or metastatic disease identified. The right ovary shows polycystic morphology. Gynecologic surgery consultation is recommended for teratoma resection in the context of confirmed anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis.

differential_by_imaging.rationale: Fat, calcification, and soft tissue in a well-circumscribed ovarian mass is the classic imaging appearance of a dermoid cyst
Small size, well-defined margins, and predominantly fatty content favor mature teratoma

recommended_actions: Gynecologic oncology or gynecologic surgery consultation for ovarian cystectomy or oophorectomy
Pathologic examination of resected specimen to confirm histology

cell_count.WBC: 62 cells/uL (elevated, decreased from 78 on initial LP)

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Positive (5 bands, unchanged from prior LP)

output.interpretation: Repeat lumbar puncture performed 10 days after initiation of immunotherapy shows improvement in inflammatory markers. WBC count has decreased from 78 to 62 cells/uL, protein has decreased from 68 to 60 mg/dL, and anti-NMDA receptor antibody titer has declined from 1:256 to 1:128. These trends suggest a partial response to immunotherapy, though persistent pleocytosis and positive antibodies indicate ongoing disease activity. Continued immunotherapy is warranted.

Osmolality Studies.clinical_significance: Low serum osmolality confirms hypotonic hyponatremia
Inappropriately concentrated urine in the setting of low serum osmolality, diagnostic of SIADH
Elevated urine sodium confirms renal sodium wasting consistent with SIADH

output.interpretation: Hypotonic hyponatremia (serum osmolality 264 mOsm/kg) with inappropriately concentrated urine (545 mOsm/kg) and elevated urine sodium (52 mEq/L) confirms the diagnosis of SIADH. This is a well-recognized complication of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis due to hypothalamic-pituitary axis involvement. Management includes fluid restriction to 1-1.2 L/day with close sodium monitoring every 6 hours. Avoid rapid correction (no more than 8 mEq/L per 24 hours) to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome.

Pre-Immunotherapy Screening.clinical_significance: No latent tuberculosis; safe for immunosuppressive therapy
Baseline IgG normal; important to monitor during and after IVIG and potential rituximab therapy
Normal IgA; IgA deficiency excluded (relevant as severe IgA deficiency is a contraindication to some IVIG products)

output.interpretation: Pre-immunotherapy screening is reassuring. All viral hepatitis and HIV serologies are negative. QuantiFERON-TB is negative. Baseline immunoglobulin levels are within normal range with adequate IgA, permitting safe IVIG administration. The patient may proceed with first-line immunotherapy (IV methylprednisolone and IVIG). If escalation to rituximab becomes necessary, these screening results also support its safe use.

differential.key_distinguishing: HSV encephalitis typically presents with acute-onset high fever, asymmetric temporal lobe hemorrhagic lesions on MRI, and positive CSF HSV PCR. In this patient, MRI shows bilateral symmetric non-hemorrhagic temporal FLAIR changes, HSV PCR is negative, and the subacute course with prominent psychiatric features and dyskinesias is more consistent with anti-NMDAR encephalitis.

First-break psychosis would not typically present with generalized seizures, orofacial dyskinesias, autonomic instability, CSF pleocytosis, or extreme delta brush on EEG. The movement disorder and seizures occurring within days of psychiatric symptom onset strongly argue against a purely psychiatric diagnosis.

Although the patient received two doses of olanzapine, NMS typically manifests with lead-pipe rigidity, very high CK (usually >1000 U/L), high sustained fever (>40 degrees Celsius), and is not associated with CSF pleocytosis or extreme delta brush on EEG. The dyskinesias in this case preceded olanzapine exposure.

Hashimoto encephalopathy requires markedly elevated anti-TPO antibodies, often with concurrent thyroid dysfunction. This patient has normal TSH, normal free T4, and normal anti-TPO antibodies, effectively excluding this diagnosis despite the family history of autoimmune thyroid disease.

Despite a maternal history of SLE and a low-titer ANA, CNS lupus typically presents with additional systemic features (rash, arthritis, serositis, renal involvement), multifocal white matter lesions, and does not cause extreme delta brush on EEG. The isolated anti-NMDAR antibody positivity with negative systemic lupus markers makes this diagnosis highly unlikely.

optimal_actions.action: Immediate stabilization: secure airway patency, establish two large-bore IV lines, continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring, and administer lorazepam 2 mg IV for seizure control
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Seizure termination, stabilization of vital signs

optimal_actions.action: Obtain urgent bloodwork: CBC, BMP, CRP, ESR, CK, hepatic panel, thyroid function tests, serum HCG, urine drug screen, blood cultures
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Hyponatremia (129 mEq/L) suggesting SIADH, elevated CK from seizures, mildly elevated inflammatory markers, normal thyroid function, negative drug screen and pregnancy test

optimal_actions.action: Order urgent brain MRI with and without gadolinium contrast
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral symmetric medial temporal lobe FLAIR hyperintensities without hemorrhage or restricted diffusion, favoring autoimmune over viral encephalitis

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture with opening pressure measurement; send CSF for cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, bacterial culture, viral PCRs (HSV, VZV, enterovirus), oligoclonal bands, IgG index, cytology, and anti-NMDA receptor antibodies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Lymphocytic pleocytosis (78 cells/uL), elevated protein, positive oligoclonal bands, elevated IgG index, negative viral PCRs, positive anti-NMDA receptor antibodies (1:256)

optimal_actions.action: Start empiric IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg every 8 hours while awaiting HSV PCR results
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Coverage for HSV encephalitis until definitively excluded by negative PCR
Extreme delta brush pattern (pathognomonic for anti-NMDAR encephalitis), frequent subclinical electrographic seizures

optimal_actions.action: Load and maintain anti-seizure medication: levetiracetam 1500 mg IV followed by 1000 mg IV every 12 hours
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Reduction in electrographic and clinical seizure burden

optimal_actions.action: Upon confirmation of anti-NMDAR antibodies: initiate first-line immunotherapy with IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 5 days concurrently with IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Initiation of immune-modulating therapy to reduce antibody-mediated neuronal damage

optimal_actions.action: Order pelvic ultrasound to evaluate for ovarian teratoma
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left ovarian teratoma identified, requiring surgical resection

optimal_actions.action: Consult gynecologic surgery for urgent teratoma resection
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Surgical planning for left ovarian cystectomy or oophorectomy to remove the immunologic trigger

optimal_actions.action: Implement fluid restriction (1.0-1.2 L/day) for SIADH with serial sodium monitoring every 6 hours
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Gradual sodium correction, avoiding more than 8 mEq/L increase per 24 hours

optimal_actions.action: If insufficient clinical response after 2 weeks of first-line immunotherapy: escalate to rituximab 375 mg/m2 weekly for 4 doses as second-line therapy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Further immune suppression through B-cell depletion

critical_actions: Perform lumbar puncture and send CSF for anti-NMDA receptor antibodies
Start empiric IV acyclovir until HSV PCR is confirmed negative
Initiate first-line immunotherapy (IV methylprednisolone plus IVIG or plasma exchange) once anti-NMDAR encephalitis is confirmed or strongly suspected
Screen for ovarian teratoma with pelvic imaging in all young women with anti-NMDAR encephalitis
Start anti-seizure medication for seizure control
Continuous EEG monitoring to detect and quantify subclinical seizures
Admit to ICU for monitoring of autonomic instability and seizure management
Discontinue olanzapine and avoid high-dose antipsychotics
contraindicated_actions: Treating as primary psychiatric disorder without first excluding autoimmune encephalitis
Administering high-dose typical or atypical antipsychotics, which can worsen dyskinesias and increase the risk of neuroleptic malignant syndrome
Delaying immunotherapy while awaiting confirmatory antibody results when clinical suspicion is high
Rapid correction of hyponatremia (exceeding 8 mEq/L per 24 hours) risking osmotic demyelination syndrome
Performing brain biopsy, which is not indicated when a treatable antibody-mediated diagnosis is confirmed
Discharging the patient from the emergency department without further workup for the underlying etiology
key_reasoning_points: The clinical triad of subacute psychiatric symptoms, new-onset seizures, and movement disorder (orofacial dyskinesias) in a young woman is the hallmark presentation of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis
Extreme delta brush on EEG is a pathognomonic finding for anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis and was originally described exclusively in this condition
The preceding viral prodrome is a well-recognized feature, occurring in approximately 70% of patients and likely representing the initial immune activation
Hyponatremia from SIADH is a common complication reflecting hypothalamic involvement and should be managed with fluid restriction, not rapid correction
Ovarian teratomas are found in approximately 40-50% of young women with anti-NMDAR encephalitis; the teratoma contains neural tissue that expresses NMDA receptors, triggering the autoimmune response
CSF anti-NMDA receptor antibodies are more sensitive and specific than serum antibodies; CSF testing is mandatory for definitive diagnosis
Brief olanzapine exposure in this case is a red herring; the dyskinesias preceded medication and are characteristic of the disease itself
Family history of autoimmune disease (maternal SLE, maternal aunt Graves disease) may reflect a genetic predisposition to autoimmunity
First-line immunotherapy should combine high-dose IV corticosteroids with IVIG or plasma exchange; early and aggressive treatment is associated with better neurological outcomes

metadata.author: NeuroBench Generator
metadata.distinguishing_features: Left-sided teratoma (vs right in S01), younger patient, PCOS comorbidity, family history of autoimmune disease, brief antipsychotic exposure as red herring, more severe hyponatremia
